verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Neither believe, nor say, nor teach, if you wish to be a catholic, that “the soul deserved to be sinful before any sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: It is, to be sure, an extremely bad desert to have deserved to be sinful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And, of course, it could not possibly have incurred so bad a desert previous to any sin, especially prior to its coming into the flesh, when it could have possessed no merit either way, either evil or good.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: How, then, can you say: “If, therefore, the soul, which could not be sinful, deserved to be sinful, it yet did not remain in sin, because as it was prefigured in Christ it was bound not to be in a sinful state, even as it was unable to be”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, just for a little consider what it is you say, and desist from repeating such a statement.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: How did the soul deserve, and how was it unable, to be sinful?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: How, I pray you tell me, did that deserve to be sinful which never lived sinfully?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: How, I ask again, was that made sinful which was not able to be sinful?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Or else, if you mean your phrase, “was unable,” to imply inability apart from the flesh, how in that case did the soul deserve to be sinful, and by reason of what desert was it sent into the flesh, when previous to its union with the flesh it was not able to be sinful, so as to deserve any evil at all?
